ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ear biscuit; mapped to broader biscuit food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers biscuits and crackers as a category; no specific ear biscuit code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for ear biscuit or equivalent snack biscuit at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of ear biscuit is approximately 30 g, equivalent to about 5–6 individual pieces, as commonly sold in small snack packets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Ear Biscuit — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo record for Ear Biscuit (F11080024). All nine reported nutrients preserved verbatim. Fields not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Biscuits and Crackers — analogous estimate based on plain sweet biscuit GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ear biscuit in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain sweet wheat-flour biscuits / crackers, which consistently return GI values in the 65–75 range in the Sydney database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 21.72) / 100 ≈ 15.2, using a 30 g serving providing 21.72 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 474 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension-sensitive snack foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid or strictly limit ear biscuit consumption; a 30 g serving delivers approximately 142 mg sodium, and regular intake can meaningfully contribute to exceeding the 2000 mg/day sodium ceiling recommended for blood-pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 21.0 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g hard glycaemic gate; high-GI refined wheat base with only 2.1 g dietary fibre per 100 g accelerates postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ear biscuit is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the combination of 21.0 g sugar, 72.4 g total carbohydrate, a GI of 70, and minimal fibre produces rapid and sustained blood glucose elevation that undermines glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (474 mg/100 g), high sugar (21.0 g/100 g), likely saturated and trans fat from shortening or lard, and ultra-processed formulation collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat ear biscuit as an occasional indulgence only; the elevated sodium, high refined-carbohydrate load, and probable saturated fat content from shortening contribute to dyslipidaemia, hypertension, and endothelial stress.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar 21.0 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g pro-inflammatory threshold; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid ear biscuit; the high sugar content, refined carbohydrate base, and AGEs produced during deep-frying collectively promote systemic inflammation and may worsen joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ear biscuit (耳朵饼, Er Duo Bing) is a traditional Singaporean and Chinese snack biscuit characterised by its distinctive oval, ear-shaped form with a visible spiral or twist in the centre. It is typically made from wheat flour, sugar, shortening or lard, eggs, and salt, then deep-fried or baked until golden and crunchy. The texture is crisp and brittle, with a mildly sweet and slightly savoury flavour profile. Ear biscuits are a popular festive and everyday snack in Singapore, commonly sold in small packets at provision shops, supermarkets, and during Chinese New Year. Nutritionally, per 100 g the biscuit provides 417 kcal, 8.6 g protein, 10.4 g fat, 72.4 g carbohydrate, 21.0 g sugar, 2.1 g dietary fibre, and 474 mg sodium. The high sugar content (21.0 g/100 g), elevated sodium (474 mg/100 g), high glycaemic index (~70), and ultra-processed formulation make it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The refined wheat flour base and minimal fibre content result in rapid glucose absorption, while the sodium level exceeds safe thresholds for blood-pressure-sensitive individuals. Clinically, ear biscuit should be treated as an occasional treat and consumed in small portions only.
common_user_queries: How many calories in ear biscuit?
Is ear biscuit suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11080024), which is an approved primary source for Singaporean foods. All nine reported nutrients are preserved verbatim. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest equivalent biscuit category, clearly documented.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for Ear Biscuit (F11080024) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific traditional Singaporean snack. Halal status is marked false as ear biscuit may contain lard (pork fat) in traditional recipes; consumers should verify product labelling. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on plain sweet wheat-flour biscuits from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for ear biscuit was found. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 30 g serving size: (70 × 21.72 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 15.2.
